docs.title: Categories
docs.text: This page describes the different categories of type system unsoundness that the checker can detect.

docs.title: runtime-modification
docs.text: Runtime code modifications that escape static type checker analysis.

docs.title: Rules in this category
docs.text:
• invalid-function-defaults - detects invalid mutation of the __defaults__ attribute of a function
  • mutating-function-code-attribute - detects mutating the __code__ attribute of a function
  • mutating-globals-dict - detects mutations to the globals() dictionary

docs.title: type-checking-suppression
docs.text: Mechanisms that suppress or bypass type checker warnings.

docs.title: Rules in this category
docs.text:
• typing-any-used - detects usage of typing.Any in type annotations
  • type-checking-directive-used - detects usage of type checking directives in comments
  • if-type-checking-used - detects usage of if TYPE_CHECKING: blocks
  • typing-cast-used - detects usage of typing.cast() function calls

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
To Enable / Disable rules, you can do the following

[tool.unsoundness-checker.rules]
typing-any-used = "ignore"

docs.title: invalid-function-defaults
docs.text:
What it does

Checks for invalid mutations of the __defaults__ attribute of a function.

Why is this bad?

Modifying the __defaults__ attribute with types different to the parameters can lead to runtime type errors.

Examples

def foo(x: int = 1) -> int:
    return x

foo.__defaults__ = ("string",)
result = foo()  # Returns "string" but type checker thinks it's int

Default level: error.

Categories: runtime-modification.

See more

docs.title: invalid-overload-implementation
docs.text:
What it does

Checks for invalid overload implementation.

Why is this bad?

Invalid overload implementation can lead to runtime errors.

Examples

from typing import overload

@overload
def foo(x: int) -> str: ...
@overload
def foo(x: str) -> int: ...
def foo(x: int | str) -> int | str:
    return x

foo("1")

Default level: error.

Categories: None.

See more

docs.title: mutating-function-code-attribute
docs.text:
What it does

Checks for mutating the __code__ attribute of a function.

Why is this bad?

Modifying the __code__ attribute allows runtime modification of function internals, which can bypass type checking and lead to runtime type errors. Type checkers cannot analyze or verify operations performed through code objects.

Examples

def foo(x: int) -> int:
    return 1

def bar(x: str) -> str:
    return "bar"

foo.__code__ = bar.__code__
# Now foo will return a string

Default level: error.

Categories: runtime-modification.

See more

docs.title: mutating-globals-dict
docs.text:
What it does

Checks for mutations to the globals() dictionary.

Why is this bad?

Modifying the globals() dictionary allows runtime modification of global variables, which can bypass type checking and lead to runtime type errors. Type checkers cannot track or verify modifications made through the globals dictionary.

Examples

x: int = 5

globals()['x'] = "hello"

# Type checker thinks `x` is an `int`, but it is now a string
result: int = x

Default level: error.

Categories: runtime-modification.

See more

docs.title: if-type-checking-used
docs.text:
What it does

Checks for usage of if TYPE_CHECKING: blocks from the typing module.

Why is this bad?

TYPE_CHECKING is False at runtime but True during static type checking. When used with an else clause where signatures don't match, the type checker validates against the if TYPE_CHECKING branch, but at runtime the else branch executes, causing runtime type errors that the type checker can't catch.

Examples

from typing import TYPE_CHECKING

if TYPE_CHECKING:
    def get_value() -> int:
        ...
else:
    def get_value() -> str:
        return "hello"

result: int = get_value()  # Type checks, but returns str at runtime!

Default level: warn.

Categories: type-checking-suppression.

See more

docs.title: type-checking-directive-used
docs.text:
What it does

Checks for usage of type checking directives in comments.

Why is this bad?

Type checking directives like # type: ignore suppress type checker warnings, which can hide potential type errors that may lead to runtime failures. These directives bypass the safety guarantees that type checking provides.

Examples

# mypy / standard (PEP 484)
x = "string" + 123  # type: ignore
y = foo()  # type: ignore[attr-defined]

Default level: warn.

Categories: type-checking-suppression.

See more

docs.title: typing-any-used
docs.text:
What it does

Checks for usage of typing.Any in type annotations.

Why is this bad?

Using typing.Any in type annotations can lead to runtime errors.

Examples

from typing import Any

def foo(x: Any) -> Any:
    return x + 1

foo("1")

Default level: warn.

Categories: type-checking-suppression.

See more

docs.title: typing-cast-used
docs.text:
What it does

Checks for usage of typing.cast() function calls.

Why is this bad?

typing.cast() tells the type checker to treat a value as a specific type without any runtime checks or validation. This can lead to runtime type errors if the cast is incorrect. Type checkers trust casts completely, so incorrect casts bypass all type safety guarantees.

Examples

from typing import cast

def get_value() -> int | str:
    return "hello"

result = cast(int, get_value())
result + 1  # Type checks, but fails at runtime!

Default level: warn.

Categories: type-checking-suppression.

See more

docs.title: typing-overload-used
docs.text:
What it does

Checks for usage of overloaded functions.

Why is this bad?

Using overloaded functions can lead to runtime errors. When users don't follow the correct overload implementation, it can lead to unexpected behavior.

Examples

from typing import overload

@overload
def foo(x: int) -> str: ...
@overload
def foo(x: str) -> int: ...
def foo(x: int | str) -> int | str:
    return x

Default level: warn.

Categories: None.

See more

docs.title: If TYPE_CHECKING used
docs.text:
Detects usage of if TYPE_CHECKING: blocks from the typing module.

TYPE_CHECKING is False at runtime but True during static type checking. This creates a disconnect between type checking and runtime behavior that can cause errors.

docs.title: Why this is bad
docs.text:
The most dangerous pattern is using if TYPE_CHECKING with an else clause where signatures don't match:

from typing import TYPE_CHECKING

if TYPE_CHECKING:
    def get_value() -> int:
        ...
else:
    def get_value() -> str:
        return "hello"
Unsoundness Checker Output 
error[if-type-checking-used]: Using `if TYPE_CHECKING:` blocks can lead to runtime errors if imports or definitions are incorrectly referenced at runtime.
 --> snippet_01.py:3:4
  |
1 | from typing import TYPE_CHECKING
2 |
3 | if TYPE_CHECKING:
  |    ^^^^^^^^^^^^^
4 |     def get_value() -> int:
5 |         ...
  |
info: rule `if-type-checking-used` was selected in the configuration file

Mypy: No Diagnostic Emitted

Pyright: No Diagnostic Emitted

from typing import TYPE_CHECKING

if TYPE_CHECKING:
    TIMEOUT: int = 30
else:
    TIMEOUT: str = "30"
Unsoundness Checker Output 
error[if-type-checking-used]: Using `if TYPE_CHECKING:` blocks can lead to runtime errors if imports or definitions are incorrectly referenced at runtime.
 --> snippet_02.py:3:4
  |
1 | from typing import TYPE_CHECKING
2 |
3 | if TYPE_CHECKING:
  |    ^^^^^^^^^^^^^
4 |     TIMEOUT: int = 30
5 | else:
  |
info: rule `if-type-checking-used` was selected in the configuration file

Mypy: No Diagnostic Emitted

Pyright: No Diagnostic Emitted

docs.title: Accessing __defaults__ attribute
docs.text:
Detects accessing the __defaults__ attribute of a function, which can bypass type checking and lead to runtime type errors.

The __defaults__ attribute stores the default values for a function's parameters. When you modify this attribute directly, type checkers cannot verify that the new defaults match the expected parameter types, potentially causing type errors at runtime.

docs.title: What gets flagged
docs.text:
def foo(x: int = 1) -> int:
    return x

foo.__defaults__ = ("string",)

foo.__defaults__ = None

def bar(x: int = 1, y: str = "string", z: bool = True) -> int:
    return x

bar.__defaults__ = (1, "string", "string")

bar.__defaults__ = None

bar.__defaults__ = (1, "string")
Unsoundness Checker Output 
error[invalid-function-defaults]: Setting `__defaults__` to an object of type `tuple[Literal["string"]]` on a function may lead to runtime type errors.
 --> snippet_01.py:4:1
  |
2 |     return x
3 |
4 | foo.__defaults__ = ("string",)
  | ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
5 |
6 | foo.__defaults__ = None
  |
info: rule `invalid-function-defaults` was selected in the configuration file

error[invalid-function-defaults]: Setting `__defaults__` to an object of type `None` on a function may lead to runtime type errors.
 --> snippet_01.py:6:1
  |
4 | foo.__defaults__ = ("string",)
5 |
6 | foo.__defaults__ = None
  | ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
7 |
8 | def bar(x: int = 1, y: str = "string", z: bool = True) -> int:
  |
info: rule `invalid-function-defaults` was selected in the configuration file

error[invalid-function-defaults]: Setting `__defaults__` to an object of type `tuple[Literal[1], Literal["string"], Literal["string"]]` on a function may lead to runtime type errors.
  --> snippet_01.py:11:1
   |
 9 |     return x
10 |
11 | bar.__defaults__ = (1, "string", "string")
   | ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
12 |
13 | bar.__defaults__ = None
   |
info: rule `invalid-function-defaults` was selected in the configuration file

error[invalid-function-defaults]: Setting `__defaults__` to an object of type `None` on a function may lead to runtime type errors.
  --> snippet_01.py:13:1
   |
11 | bar.__defaults__ = (1, "string", "string")
12 |
13 | bar.__defaults__ = None
   | ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
14 |
15 | bar.__defaults__ = (1, "string")
   |
info: rule `invalid-function-defaults` was selected in the configuration file

error[invalid-function-defaults]: Setting `__defaults__` to an object of type `tuple[Literal[1], Literal["string"]]` on a function may lead to runtime type errors.
  --> snippet_01.py:15:1
   |
13 | bar.__defaults__ = None
14 |
15 | bar.__defaults__ = (1, "string")
   | ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
   |
info: rule `invalid-function-defaults` was selected in the configuration file

Mypy: No Diagnostic Emitted

Pyright: No Diagnostic Emitted

We currently do not emit an error for this case, in future we will not

We do not emit errors if the types of the new defaults match the expected parameter types.

def foo(x: str = "string") -> str:
    return x

# This is type safe
foo.__defaults__ = ("another_string",)

foo.__defaults__ = ("another_string", "another_string")

def bar(x: int): ...

bar.__defaults__ = (1,)

def baz(x: int): ...

baz.__defaults__ = None

Unsoundness Checker: No Diagnostic Emitted

Mypy: No Diagnostic Emitted

Pyright: No Diagnostic Emitted

docs.title: Valid Overloads
docs.text:
from typing import overload

@overload
def foo(x: int) -> str: ...
@overload
def foo(x: str) -> int: ...
def foo(x: int | str) -> str | int:
    if isinstance(x, int):
        return str(x)
    return len(x)

Unsoundness Checker: No Diagnostic Emitted

Mypy: No Diagnostic Emitted

Pyright: No Diagnostic Emitted

from typing import overload

@overload
def foo(x: int, y: str) -> bool: ...
@overload
def foo(x: str, y: int) -> float: ...
def foo(x: int | str, y: str | int) -> bool | float:
    if isinstance(x, int) and isinstance(y, str):
        return True
    return 1.0

Unsoundness Checker: No Diagnostic Emitted

Mypy: No Diagnostic Emitted

Pyright: No Diagnostic Emitted

docs.title: What we can catch
docs.text:
We emit diagnostics for return types that are not assignable to the union of the overload return types.

The main issue that type checkers won't always pick up on is that the implementation return type can simply be object which everything is assignable to.

from typing import overload

@overload
def bar(x: int) -> str: ...
@overload
def bar(x: str) -> int: ...
def bar(x: int | str) -> object:
    return b""
Unsoundness Checker Output 
error[invalid-overload-implementation]: Invalid overload implementation can lead to runtime errors.
 --> snippet_03.py:8:5
  |
6 | def bar(x: str) -> int: ...
7 | def bar(x: int | str) -> object:
8 |     return b""
  |     ^^^^^^^^^^
  |
info: This overload implementation is invalid as `Literal[b""]` is not assignable to any of the overload return types (`str`, `int`)
info: rule `invalid-overload-implementation` was selected in the configuration file

Mypy: No Diagnostic Emitted

Pyright: No Diagnostic Emitted

A side note is that if you changed the return type of bar implementation to int | str, then most type checkers would catch this error.

from typing import overload

@overload
def bar(x: int) -> str: ...
@overload
def bar(x: str) -> int: ...
def bar(x: int | str) -> int | str:
    return b""
Unsoundness Checker Output 
error[invalid-overload-implementation]: Invalid overload implementation can lead to runtime errors.
 --> snippet_04.py:8:5
  |
6 | def bar(x: str) -> int: ...
7 | def bar(x: int | str) -> int | str:
8 |     return b""
  |     ^^^^^^^^^^
  |
info: This overload implementation is invalid as `Literal[b""]` is not assignable to any of the overload return types (`str`, `int`)
info: rule `invalid-overload-implementation` was selected in the configuration file
Mypy Output 
<temp_dir>/snippet_04.py:8: error: Incompatible return value type (got "bytes", expected "int | str")  [return-value]
Found 1 error in 1 file (checked 1 source file)
Pyright Output 
<temp_dir>/snippet_04.py
  <temp_dir>/snippet_04.py:8:12 - error: Type "Literal[b""]" is not assignable to return type "int | str"
    Type "Literal[b""]" is not assignable to type "int | str"
      "Literal[b""]" is not assignable to "int"
      "Literal[b""]" is not assignable to "str" (reportReturnType)
1 error, 0 warnings, 0 informations

docs.title: What we can't catch
docs.text:
Due to more complex examples, we currently can't catch all invalid overload implementations.

The idea for the implementation of this was that at each return statement if all input variables had been narrowed to the types of the matching overload statement, then this would be a valid implementation.

This is a obvious incorrect implementation.

from typing import overload

@overload
def baz(x: int) -> str: ...
@overload
def baz(x: str) -> int: ...
def baz(x: int | str) -> int | str:
    return x

Unsoundness Checker: No Diagnostic Emitted

Mypy: No Diagnostic Emitted

Pyright: No Diagnostic Emitted

This is a more complex example which makes it very difficult to catch any errors. this is a valid implementation, but because we lost information about the use of x, we cannot make any assumptions about if this is valid or not.

And because we don't want to emit false positives, we will emit anything here.

from typing import overload, TypeVar

T = TypeVar("T")

def custom_copy(x: T) -> T:
    return x

@overload
def baz(x: list[int]) -> str: ...
@overload
def baz(x: str) -> int: ...
def baz(x: list[int] | str) -> int | str:
    # Some complex function that loses information about the type of x, and how it is used
    y = custom_copy(x)
    if isinstance(y, list):
        return ""
    else:
        return 1

Unsoundness Checker: No Diagnostic Emitted

Mypy: No Diagnostic Emitted

Pyright: No Diagnostic Emitted

docs.title: Mutating __code__ attribute
docs.text:
Detects setting the __code__ attribute of a function, which can bypass type checking and lead to runtime type errors.

The __code__ attribute contains the compiled bytecode of a function. When you modify this attribute directly, you can completely change the function's behavior, including its parameter types and return type, without the type checker being aware of the change.

docs.title: What gets flagged
docs.text:
def foo(x: int) -> int:
    return 1

def bar(x: str) -> str:
    return "!"

foo.__code__ = bar.__code__

# Type checker things `result` is an `int`, but it is a string
result = foo(10)
Unsoundness Checker Output 
error[mutating-function-code-attribute]: Mutating `__code__` attribute on a function may lead to runtime type errors.
 --> snippet_01.py:7:1
  |
5 |     return "!"
6 |
7 | foo.__code__ = bar.__code__
  | ^^^^^^^^^^^^
8 |
9 | # Type checker things `result` is an `int`, but it is a string
  |
info: rule `mutating-function-code-attribute` was selected in the configuration file

Mypy: No Diagnostic Emitted

Pyright: No Diagnostic Emitted

docs.title: Mutating globals() dict
docs.text:
Detects mutations to the globals() dictionary, which can bypass type checking and lead to runtime type errors.

The globals() function returns a dictionary representing the current global symbol table. When you modify this dictionary directly, you can change the types of global variables at runtime without the type checker being aware of the change.

docs.title: Mutating globals() dictionary
docs.text:
x: int = 5

globals()['x'] = "hello"

# Type checker thinks `result` is an `int`, but it is a string
result: int = x
Unsoundness Checker Output 
error[mutating-globals-dict]: Mutating the `globals()` dictionary may lead to runtime type errors.
 --> snippet_01.py:3:1
  |
1 | x: int = 5
2 |
3 | globals()['x'] = "hello"
  | ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
4 |
5 | # Type checker thinks `result` is an `int`, but it is a string
  |
info: rule `mutating-globals-dict` was selected in the configuration file

Mypy: No Diagnostic Emitted

Pyright: No Diagnostic Emitted

docs.title: Type checking directive used
docs.text:
Detects usage of type checking directives in comments, which suppress type checker warnings and may hide potential type errors.

Type checking directives like # type: ignore tell type checkers to skip validation of specific lines or sections of code. While sometimes necessary, they can mask real bugs that would otherwise be caught during static analysis.

docs.title: What gets flagged
docs.text: We detect directives from the major Python type checkers:

docs.title: mypy / Standard (PEP 484)
docs.text:
# Blanket ignore - suppresses all type errors on this line
result = unsafe_operation()  # type: ignore

# Specific error code - suppresses only specific error types
x = get_value()  # type: ignore[attr-defined]
y = compute()  # type: ignore[arg-type]
Unsoundness Checker Output 
error[type-checking-directive-used]: Type checking directive `type: ignore` suppresses type checker warnings, which may hide potential type errors.
 --> snippet_01.py:2:30
  |
1 | # Blanket ignore - suppresses all type errors on this line
2 | result = unsafe_operation()  # type: ignore
  |                              ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
3 |
4 | # Specific error code - suppresses only specific error types
  |
info: rule `type-checking-directive-used` was selected in the configuration file

error[type-checking-directive-used]: Type checking directive `type: ignore` suppresses type checker warnings, which may hide potential type errors.
 --> snippet_01.py:5:18
  |
4 | # Specific error code - suppresses only specific error types
5 | x = get_value()  # type: ignore[attr-defined]
  |                  ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
6 | y = compute()  # type: ignore[arg-type]
  |
info: rule `type-checking-directive-used` was selected in the configuration file

error[type-checking-directive-used]: Type checking directive `type: ignore` suppresses type checker warnings, which may hide potential type errors.
 --> snippet_01.py:6:16
  |
4 | # Specific error code - suppresses only specific error types
5 | x = get_value()  # type: ignore[attr-defined]
6 | y = compute()  # type: ignore[arg-type]
  |                ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  |
info: rule `type-checking-directive-used` was selected in the configuration file

docs.title: pyright
docs.text:
# Blanket ignore
data = risky_call()  # pyright: ignore

# Specific report type
value = process()  # pyright: ignore[reportOptionalMemberAccess]
item = fetch()  # pyright: ignore[reportGeneralTypeIssues]
Unsoundness Checker Output 
error[type-checking-directive-used]: Type checking directive `pyright: ignore` suppresses type checker warnings, which may hide potential type errors.
 --> snippet_02.py:2:22
  |
1 | # Blanket ignore
2 | data = risky_call()  # pyright: ignore
  |                      ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
3 |
4 | # Specific report type
  |
info: rule `type-checking-directive-used` was selected in the configuration file

error[type-checking-directive-used]: Type checking directive `pyright: ignore` suppresses type checker warnings, which may hide potential type errors.
 --> snippet_02.py:5:20
  |
4 | # Specific report type
5 | value = process()  # pyright: ignore[reportOptionalMemberAccess]
  |                    ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
6 | item = fetch()  # pyright: ignore[reportGeneralTypeIssues]
  |
info: rule `type-checking-directive-used` was selected in the configuration file

error[type-checking-directive-used]: Type checking directive `pyright: ignore` suppresses type checker warnings, which may hide potential type errors.
 --> snippet_02.py:6:17
  |
4 | # Specific report type
5 | value = process()  # pyright: ignore[reportOptionalMemberAccess]
6 | item = fetch()  # pyright: ignore[reportGeneralTypeIssues]
  |                 ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  |
info: rule `type-checking-directive-used` was selected in the configuration file

docs.title: ty
docs.text:
result = unsafe_operation()  # ty: ignore[unresolved-import]
data = get_data()  # ty: ignore[attr-defined]
Unsoundness Checker Output 
error[type-checking-directive-used]: Type checking directive `ty: ignore` suppresses type checker warnings, which may hide potential type errors.
 --> snippet_03.py:1:30
  |
1 | result = unsafe_operation()  # ty: ignore[unresolved-import]
  |                              ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
2 | data = get_data()  # ty: ignore[attr-defined]
  |
info: rule `type-checking-directive-used` was selected in the configuration file

error[type-checking-directive-used]: Type checking directive `ty: ignore` suppresses type checker warnings, which may hide potential type errors.
 --> snippet_03.py:2:20
  |
1 | result = unsafe_operation()  # ty: ignore[unresolved-import]
2 | data = get_data()  # ty: ignore[attr-defined]
  |                    ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  |
info: rule `type-checking-directive-used` was selected in the configuration file

docs.title: pyrefly
docs.text:
value = process()  # pyrefly: ignore[unused-import]
item = fetch()  # pyrefly: ignore[type-error]
Unsoundness Checker Output 
error[type-checking-directive-used]: Type checking directive `pyrefly: ignore` suppresses type checker warnings, which may hide potential type errors.
 --> snippet_04.py:1:20
  |
1 | value = process()  # pyrefly: ignore[unused-import]
  |                    ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
2 | item = fetch()  # pyrefly: ignore[type-error]
  |
info: rule `type-checking-directive-used` was selected in the configuration file

error[type-checking-directive-used]: Type checking directive `pyrefly: ignore` suppresses type checker warnings, which may hide potential type errors.
 --> snippet_04.py:2:17
  |
1 | value = process()  # pyrefly: ignore[unused-import]
2 | item = fetch()  # pyrefly: ignore[type-error]
  |                 ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  |
info: rule `type-checking-directive-used` was selected in the configuration file

docs.title: Why this matters
docs.text:
Type checking directives bypass the safety guarantees that static type checking provides. For example:

def calculate_total(items: list[int]) -> int:
    return sum(items)

# This will fail at runtime, but the directive hides the error
result = calculate_total("not a list")  # type: ignore
Unsoundness Checker Output 
error[type-checking-directive-used]: Type checking directive `type: ignore` suppresses type checker warnings, which may hide potential type errors.
 --> snippet_05.py:5:41
  |
4 | # This will fail at runtime, but the directive hides the error
5 | result = calculate_total("not a list")  # type: ignore
  |                                         ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  |
info: rule `type-checking-directive-used` was selected in the configuration file

Instead of suppressing the error, fix the underlying type issue:

def calculate_total(items: list[int]) -> int:
    return sum(items)

# Correct the type to match the function signature
result = calculate_total([1, 2, 3])

Unsoundness Checker: No Diagnostic Emitted

docs.title: When directives might be necessary
docs.text:
There are legitimate cases where type checking directives are needed: - Working with dynamic third-party libraries that lack type stubs - Handling complex metaprogramming that confuses type checkers - Temporary workarounds during incremental type adoption

However, these should be rare and well-documented exceptions, not the norm.

docs.title: typing.Any used
docs.text:
Detects usage of typing.Any in type annotations, which can lead to runtime errors by bypassing type checking.

When you use Any, you're essentially telling the type checker "this could be anything," which defeats the purpose of having types in the first place. This can cause bugs that would otherwise be caught at development time.

docs.title: What gets flagged
docs.text: We currently detect Any in these places:

docs.title: Function parameters
docs.text:
from typing import Any

def process_data(data: Any) -> str:
    return str(data)
Unsoundness Checker Output 
error[typing-any-used]: Using `typing.Any` in type annotations can lead to runtime errors.
 --> snippet_01.py:3:24
  |
1 | from typing import Any
2 |
3 | def process_data(data: Any) -> str:
  |                        ^^^
4 |     return str(data)
  |
info: rule `typing-any-used` was selected in the configuration file

Mypy: No Diagnostic Emitted

Pyright: No Diagnostic Emitted

docs.title: Return types
docs.text:
from typing import Any

def get_user_data() -> Any:
    return {"name": "John", "age": 30}
Unsoundness Checker Output 
error[typing-any-used]: Using `typing.Any` in type annotations can lead to runtime errors.
 --> snippet_02.py:3:24
  |
1 | from typing import Any
2 |
3 | def get_user_data() -> Any:
  |                        ^^^
4 |     return {"name": "John", "age": 30}
  |
info: rule `typing-any-used` was selected in the configuration file

Mypy: No Diagnostic Emitted

Pyright: No Diagnostic Emitted

docs.title: Variable assignments
docs.text:
from typing import Any

def get_user() -> dict[str, str | int]:
    return {"name": "John", "age": 30}

user_info: Any = get_user()
Unsoundness Checker Output 
error[typing-any-used]: Using `typing.Any` in type annotations can lead to runtime errors.
 --> snippet_03.py:6:12
  |
4 |     return {"name": "John", "age": 30}
5 |
6 | user_info: Any = get_user()
  |            ^^^
  |
info: rule `typing-any-used` was selected in the configuration file

Mypy: No Diagnostic Emitted

Pyright: No Diagnostic Emitted

docs.title: Why this matters
docs.text:
Using Any removes type safety. For example:

from typing import Any

def calculate_total(items: Any) -> int:
    return sum(item for item in items)
Unsoundness Checker Output 
error[typing-any-used]: Using `typing.Any` in type annotations can lead to runtime errors.
 --> snippet_04.py:3:28
  |
1 | from typing import Any
2 |
3 | def calculate_total(items: Any) -> int:
  |                            ^^^
4 |     return sum(item for item in items)
  |
info: rule `typing-any-used` was selected in the configuration file

Mypy: No Diagnostic Emitted

Pyright: No Diagnostic Emitted

Instead, be specific about what you expect:

def calculate_total(items: list[int]) -> int:
    return sum(item for item in items)

Unsoundness Checker: No Diagnostic Emitted

Mypy: No Diagnostic Emitted

Pyright: No Diagnostic Emitted

docs.title: typing.cast used
docs.text:
Detects usage of typing.cast() function calls, which can lead to runtime errors by bypassing type checking without any runtime validation.

typing.cast() tells the type checker to treat a value as a specific type without performing any runtime checks. Type checkers trust casts completely, so incorrect casts bypass all type safety guarantees.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
from typing import cast

def foo() -> int:
    return cast(int, "hello")
Unsoundness Checker Output 
error[typing-cast-used]: Using `typing.cast()` bypasses type checking and can lead to runtime type errors.
 --> snippet_01.py:4:12
  |
3 | def foo() -> int:
4 |     return cast(int, "hello")
  |            ^^^^
  |
info: Consider using `isinstance` checks to ensure types at runtime.
info: rule `typing-cast-used` was selected in the configuration file

Mypy: No Diagnostic Emitted

Pyright: No Diagnostic Emitted

docs.title: Better alternative
docs.text:
Instead of using cast(), use assert isinstance() to validate types at runtime:

def get_value() -> int | str:
    return "hello"

result = get_value()
assert isinstance(result, int)
# Now we know it's safe - the assertion will catch incorrect types

Unsoundness Checker: No Diagnostic Emitted

Mypy: No Diagnostic Emitted

Pyright: No Diagnostic Emitted

This provides actual runtime safety instead of just lying to the type checker.

docs.title: typing.overload used
docs.text:
Detects use of overloaded functions.

Overloaded functions can often lead to runtime errors if the implementations are not consistent with the overload definitions.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
Here is an example of an overloaded function that is implemented correctly:

from typing import overload

@overload
def add(a: int, b: int) -> int: ...

@overload
def add(a: float, b: float) -> float: ...

def add(a: float, b: float) -> float | int:
    return a + b
Unsoundness Checker Output 
error[typing-overload-used]: Using `typing.overload` can lead to runtime errors.
 --> snippet_01.py:3:1
  |
1 | from typing import overload
2 |
3 | @overload
  | ^^^^^^^^^
4 | def add(a: int, b: int) -> int: ...
  |
info: rule `typing-overload-used` was selected in the configuration file

error[typing-overload-used]: Using `typing.overload` can lead to runtime errors.
 --> snippet_01.py:6:1
  |
4 | def add(a: int, b: int) -> int: ...
5 |
6 | @overload
  | ^^^^^^^^^
7 | def add(a: float, b: float) -> float: ...
  |
info: rule `typing-overload-used` was selected in the configuration file

Mypy: No Diagnostic Emitted

Pyright: No Diagnostic Emitted

Whereas this one is not:

from typing import overload

@overload
def foo(x: int) -> str: ...

@overload
def foo(x: str) -> int: ...

def foo(x: int | str) -> str | int:
    return x
Unsoundness Checker Output 
error[typing-overload-used]: Using `typing.overload` can lead to runtime errors.
 --> snippet_02.py:3:1
  |
1 | from typing import overload
2 |
3 | @overload
  | ^^^^^^^^^
4 | def foo(x: int) -> str: ...
  |
info: rule `typing-overload-used` was selected in the configuration file

error[typing-overload-used]: Using `typing.overload` can lead to runtime errors.
 --> snippet_02.py:6:1
  |
4 | def foo(x: int) -> str: ...
5 |
6 | @overload
  | ^^^^^^^^^
7 | def foo(x: str) -> int: ...
  |
info: rule `typing-overload-used` was selected in the configuration file

Mypy: No Diagnostic Emitted

Pyright: No Diagnostic Emitted

It is very hard for us to detect when an overloaded function is implemented incorrectly, so with this rule we can try to eliminate all uses of overloaded functions.
